coredata.dc:title: LED irradiation of halogen/nitrogen-doped polymeric graphene quantum dots triggers the photodynamic inactivation of bacteria in infected wounds
coredata.dc:creator: Huang, Hao-Hsin
Anand, Anisha
Lin, Chin-Jung

Lin, Yang-Wei
Harroun, Scott G.
Huang, Chih-Ching
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging disinfectants, owing to their distinctive photodynamic and antibacterial activities. Halogen-based antimicrobial materials are also known to be effective against infectious pathogens. In this work, we have developed visible light-activatable halogen/nitrogen co-doped polymeric graphene quantum dots [X/N–PGQDs, (X = Cl, Br, I)] as an antibacterial agent, prepared from spermidine and hydrohalic acid by a simple one-step heating method. We demonstrate that these high positively charged X/N–PGQDs are effective antibacterial agents (minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) less than 0.5 μg mL−1) for the inhibition of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The antibacterial activities of X/N–PGQDs are enhanced upon exposure to white LED light irradiation due to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) which induce oxidative stress, as well as physical disruption of the bacteria. Moreover, the MIC of X/N–PGQDs with LED light irradiation is 100-fold lower than in the dark, revealing their strong photo-induced inactivation of bacteria. X/N–PGQDs are effective for the eradication of multidrug-resistant bacteria in infectious wounds, even with just 1-min of irradiation. With their negligible cytotoxicity, we believe that X/N–PGQDs have great potential as a visible light-activated antibacterial nanomaterial for preclinical application in wound healing.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis and characterization of halogen/nitrogen co-doped polymeric graphene quantum dots (X/N–pgqds)
Bacterial growth and light-assisted bactericidal inactivation assays

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Science and Technology, Taiwan

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan under Contract Nos. 107-2622-B-182-001-CC2 , 107-2113-M-019-004-MY3 , 107-2622-M-019-001-CC2 , 107-2627-M-007-007-MY3 and 108-2638-M-002-001-MY2 , and the Center of Excellence for the Oceans, National Taiwan Ocean University from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan. The assistance of Ms. Ya-Yun Yang and Ms. Ching-Yen Lin from the Instrument Center of National Taiwan University (NTU) for TEM measurement is appreciated.

To determine the concentration at which the functional X/N−PGQDs could be used without significant side effects, we investigated the cytotoxicity of Cl/N–PGQDs-270, Br/N–PGQDs-240 and I/N–PGQDs-210 toward HaCaT (immortalized human keratinocyte cell line) cells. Owing to the long-lived, spontaneously immortalized human keratinocyte line, HaCaT cells are widely used in scientific research for the characterization of human keratinocyte. Cytotoxicity was assessed using the Prestoblue cell-viability assay. We examined the cell viability of HaCaT cells treated with Cl/N–PGQDs-270, Br/N–PGQDs-240 or I/N–PGQDs-210 at different concentrations (1–50 μg mL−1) (Fig. S14, Supporting Information). The X/N−PGQDs are scarcely toxic to the cells (cell viability >90%) at concentration which is 10000-fold higher (50 μg mL−1) than the lowest MIC value for the tested bacteria. Furthermore, no significant hemolytic reaction was observed, even with up to 50 μg mL−1 (Fig. S15, Supporting Information). Our results reveal that the X/N−PGQDs exhibit significant antibacterial activity, while displaying negligible cytotoxicity toward mammalian cells, as well as no hemolytic effect toward red blood cells (RBCs), which is indispensable for a potential photobactericidal candidate. It is important to consider that bacterial and mammalian cells differ widely in lipid membrane composition and transmembrane potential. Bacterial membranes are composed of negatively charged phospholipids, whereas the mammalian cells possess fewer charges and mainly consist of cholesterol and phospholipids. The interaction of the highly positively charged X/N−PGQDs with the negatively charged bacterial membrane is faster when compared with the mammalian cells [20], and therefore, the mammalian cells have higher tolerance toward the X/N−PGQDs.This work was supported by the Ministry of Science and Technology of Taiwan under Contract Nos. 107-2622-B-182-001-CC2, 107-2113-M-019-004-MY3, 107-2622-M-019-001-CC2, 107-2627-M-007-007-MY3 and 108-2638-M-002-001-MY2, and the Center of Excellence for the Oceans, National Taiwan Ocean University from The Featured Areas Research Center Program within the framework of the Higher Education Sprout Project by the Ministry of Education (MOE) in Taiwan. The assistance of Ms. Ya-Yun Yang and Ms. Ching-Yen Lin from the Instrument Center of National Taiwan University (NTU) for TEM measurement is appreciated.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of the strategy for (A) the synthesis of halogen (X) and nitrogen (N) co-doped polymeric graphene quantum dots (X/N–PGQDs) and (B) their application for visible light-assisted bacterial inactivation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) TEM images, (B) UV–vis absorption spectra, and (C) fluorescence spectra of (a) Cl/N–PGQDs, (b) Br/N–PGQDs, and (c) I/N–PGQDs. The concentration of purified PGQDs for UV–Vis absorption and fluorescence measurements is 10 μg mL−1. Insets to (A): size distribution and HRTEM of the PGQDs. Inset to (B) and (C): photographs of the corresponding PGQDs solutions (B) before and (C) after excitation under a hand-held UV lamp (365 nm). (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Representative colony formation of E. coli and S. aureus on LB agar plates in the absence and presence of spermidine, HCl/spermidine (0.005 μg mL−1; in term of spermidine) or Cl/N-PGQDs-270 (0.005 μg mL−1) without or with white LED irradiation (∼350 W m−2) for 1 min. (B) Comparison of MIC of spermidine, HCl/spermidine, HBr/spermidine, HI/spermidine, Cl/N–PGQDs, Br/N–PGQDs and I/N–PGQDs (synthesized at 210, 240 or 270 °C) without and with white LED irradiation (∼350 W m−2) against E. coli. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(a) Bright-field and fluorescent images of (A) E. coli (107 CFU mL−1) and (B) S. aureus (107 CFU mL−1) under excitation with (b) UV light (360–380 nm), (c) blue light (460–480 nm), and (d) green light (510–530 nm) after incubation with the X/N–PGQDs (50 μg mL−1) in physiological buffer solution [PBS; 25 mM Tris–HCl (pH 7.4), 150 mM NaCl, 5.0 mM KCl, 1.0 mM MgCl2, and 1.0 mM CaCl2] for 1 min. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

TEM images of (A) E. coli cells and (B) S. aureus cells (a) before treatment and (b–d) after treatment with (b) Cl/N–PGQDs-270 (50 μg mL−1), (c) Br/N–PGQDs-240 (50 μg mL−1), and (d) I/N–PGQDs-210 (50 μg mL−1) with white LED irradiation (350 W m−2) for 1 min. The red arrows indicate significant disruption of the bacterial membranes. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)

(A) Photographs of MRSA-infected wounds treated with Cl/N−PGQDs-270 without or with 1 min LED light (350 W m−2). The infected wounds receiving PBS without LED light irradiation serve as the control group. (B) A plot of relative wound sizes vs. treatment time. Error bars represent the standard deviation of four repeated measurements. (C) Photographs of bacterial cultures from the tissue of untreated (PBS only) and treated wounds on day 3 post-operation. (A colour version of this figure can be viewed online.)
MIC and PDI values of X/N-PGQDs for E. coli and S. aureus.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are emerging disinfectants, owing to their distinctive photodynamic and antibacterial activities. Halogen-based antimicrobial materials are also known to be effective against infectious pathogens. In this work, we have developed visible light-activatable halogen/nitrogen co-doped polymeric graphene quantum dots [X/N–PGQDs, (X = Cl, Br, I)] as an antibacterial agent, prepared from spermidine and hydrohalic acid by a simple one-step heating method. We demonstrate that these high positively charged X/N–PGQDs are effective antibacterial agents (minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) less than 0.5 μg mL−1) for the inhibition of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The antibacterial activities of X/N–PGQDs are enhanced upon exposure to white LED light irradiation due to the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) which induce oxidative stress, as well as physical disruption of the bacteria. Moreover, the MIC of X/N–PGQDs with LED light irradiation is 100-fold lower than in the dark, revealing their strong photo-induced inactivation of bacteria. X/N–PGQDs are effective for the eradication of multidrug-resistant bacteria in infectious wounds, even with just 1-min of irradiation. With their negligible cytotoxicity, we believe that X/N–PGQDs have great potential as a visible light-activated antibacterial nanomaterial for preclinical application in wound healing.

ce:section.ce:para: Microbial infections arising from bacteria and fungi pose serious threats to human health. Pathogenic bacteria play a major role in causing various diseases and infections, such as pneumonia, endocarditis, osteomyelitis, gastroenteritis, cellulitis, and toxic shock syndrome [1]. For example, severe bacterial colonization of wounds is potentially fatal, since differentiation of fibroblasts is impaired and the normal tissues and the immune system are undermined [2]. Bacterial infections are not only harmful to human health but also cause infectious diseases in farming, forestry, animal husbandry, and fishery [3]. To eliminate infectious bacteria, conventional antibiotics have been widely used due to their cost-effectiveness and powerful outcomes [4]. However, the extensive use and misuse of antibiotics have resulted in potential health risks and financial burdens due to the developing drug resistance [5]. As an alternative to antibiotics, recent researches are focused on nanoscale materials with intrinsic antimicrobial activity due to their unique properties, such as specific surface characteristics, induced oxidative stress, and facile functionalization [6–11]. Metal-based nanoparticles like silver, gold, copper oxide, zinc oxide, and titanium oxide nanoparticles, have been reported to exhibit promising antimicrobial activities [9–11]. These metal-based nanoparticles interact with the cell membranes of bacteria to interfere the electron transport chain, which disturbs the permeability and respiratory function of the bacteria. They also destroy the DNA and proteins of the bacteria by the release of metal ions and/or generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) [11–13].

In spite of their antibacterial properties, the biocompatibility concerns of metal-based nanomaterials have opened the door for alternative carbon-based antibacterial agents [14]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) form a major group of nanomaterials used for a wide range of applications, due to their small size, ease of synthesis and surface modification, and high biocompatibility [15–18]. CQDs have been widely studied in recent years for the detection and eradication of bacteria due to their excellent fluorescent and antibacterial properties [17]. The antimicrobial activity of CQDs is dependent on their sizes, structures, charges, and surface functional groups, in addition to the nature of the targeted microbe [19–27]. The desired optical and physicochemical properties may be imparted to the CQDs by varying the precursor and also through surface functionalization. The antibacterial activities of CQDs rely on their ability to disrupt the bacterial membrane and also the generation of ROS to induce oxidative stress. Positively charged CQDs are reported to interact with the bacterial membrane through electrostatic interaction leading to their disruption [17,28–30]. Apart from the physical interaction of bacteria with the CQDs, a combined photodynamic effect can boost their antibacterial properties [31–36]. The photo-illumination enhances the ROS generation resulting in irreversible damage to the bacterial cells [31–38]. The amount of ROS generated, however, depends on the intensity and wavelength of the irradiated light.

Unlike CQDs, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) possess prominent graphene-like structure in its core imparting the properties of graphene, however, exhibiting less toxicity [18]. Photoexcitation of GQDs generates ROS for elevating oxidative stress to bacterial cells [39]. Heteroatom-doped GQDs or those modified with other nanomaterials have been reported to increase the electronic density and charge transfer ability of the GQDs, thereby enhancing their ability to generate ROS under photo-illumination [32,33,40,41]. Wang et al. have reported the improved singlet oxygen-generating efficiency of GQDs upon doping with chlorine, which in turn contributes to their antibacterial efficiency [32]. In addition, light irradiation of heteroatom co-doped GQDs can further promote the formation of free radicals and generate heat, which cause irreversible damage to the bacterial membrane, thereby causing cytoplasmic leakage and cell death [25,32,33]. Although many nanomaterial-based photodynamic antimicrobial therapies have been developed for improving wound healing, they usually require expensive light sources such as xenon arc lamp and laser with long irradiation time. The long time for wound treatment may increase the risk of recurrent infections. Thus, to accelerate wound healing and to reduce the risk of wound infection, this study developed a rapid and effective platform for antibacterial application, by employing LED-assisted bactericidal inactivation with only 1-min irradiation via halogen/nitrogen co-doped polymeric graphene quantum dots (X/N-PGQDs; Fig. 1 ). The ROS generated through the photodynamic therapy elevates the antibacterial ability of the synthesized X/N–PGQDs for infected wound treatment. With a very low concentration and negligible cytotoxicity, X/N–PGQDs can be used as a promising antibacterial agent to minimize sepsis caused by bacterial wound infections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis and characterization of halogen/nitrogen co-doped polymeric graphene quantum dots (X/N–pgqds)
ce:section.ce:para: Halogen/nitrogen co-doped polymeric graphene quantum dots (X/N–PGQDs) were synthesized by a modified procedure reported in our previous studies [19,20]. Briefly, the procedure involves heating of a mixture (1 mL) of spermidine (4 mg mL−1; ∼0.13 M) and hydrochloric acid (0.4 M), hydrobromic acid (0.4 M) or hydroiodic acid (0.4 M) in a glass vial at 210–300 °C in a muffle furnace for 3 h. The residue thus obtained was cooled to room temperature before dissolving in deionized (DI) water (5 mL), and sonication for 1 h, followed by the removal of large particles by centrifugation at the relative centrifugal force (RCF) of 5000 g for 30 min. Discarding the residue, the supernatant was collected and dialyzed against DI water through a dialysis membrane with a molecular weight cutoff 0.5–1.0 kDa (Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories) for 12 h. The DI water was replaced every 2 h and the purified X/N-PGQDs solutions were stored at 4 °C. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the X/N–PGQDs were recorded using a Tecnai-F20-G2 (Philips/FEI, Hillsboro, OR, USA) system operating at 200 kV. Diluted solutions of X/N-PGQDs (0.01 mg mL−1) were dropped onto carbon-coated copper grids (300-mesh) and dried under vacuum, for TEM measurements. Atomic force microscopy (AFM) images of X/N–PGQDs were recorded using Shimadzu SPM-9600 AFM (Shimadzu Co, Kyoto, Japan). For AFM measurements, the samples (0.01 mg mL−1) were dropped on silicon wafer and dried overnight under vacuum prior to the measurements. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) (Zetasizer 3000HS analyzer, Malvern Instruments, Malvern, UK) was used to measure the zeta potential and the hydrodynamic diameter of the X/N–PGQDs. The samples (50 μg mL−1) in 5 mM sodium phosphate (pH 7.4) and phosphate buffer saline (PBS; containing 10 mM Na2HPO4, 2.0 mM KH2PO4, 137 mM NaCl, 0.5 mM CaCl2 and 1.0 mM MgCl2; pH = 7.4) were used for the DLS measurements. The duration of measurement runs is set as 10, and the output value obtained is the average of the 10 runs. Three such measurements were conducted and the average value is taken. A SynergyTM 4 multi-mode microplate reader was used (Biotek Instruments, Winooski, VT, USA) to measure the absorption of the X/N–PGQDs in ultrapure water and their fluorescence spectra at excitation wavelengths between 325 and 445 nm. X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of the X/N–PGQDs were recorded using a D/MAX 2200 VPC (Rigaku, Tokyo, Japan). Functional groups in the X/N–PGQDs were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy with a FT/IR-6100 (JASCO, Tokyo, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bacterial growth and light-assisted bactericidal inactivation assays
ce:section.ce:para: The bacterial growth assay was carried out using the protocol mentioned in our previous reports [19,20,26]. Escherichia coli (E. coli), Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus), and Staphylococcus epidermidis (S. epidermidis) were grown in Lennox broth (LB) media. The bacterial cultures were incubated at 37 °C under shaking at 250 rpm. A portion of the cell mixture (1.0 mL, OD600 = 1.0) was centrifuged at 3000 g for 10 min at 25 °C and washed (2 times) with 5 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, 1.0 mL). The bacterial strains were tested for the minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of the X/N–PGQDs, using the broth microdilution method. E. coli and S. aureus (104 CFU mL−1) were incubated with X/N–PGQDs (in 5 mM SODIUM PHOSPHATE, pH 7.4) for 1 h under shaking at 250 rpm and all solutions were exposed to LED light (350 W m−2) for 1 min, along with solution without light exposure (dark control), without PGQDs (light control), and without PGQDs and light (negative control). Then, the bacterial suspension was diluted and 100 μL was spread on LB agar plates, followed by incubation for 12 h, at 37 °C. The lowest concentration of X/N–PGQDs that could completely inhibit (>99%) the bacterial growth, compared to the control was reported as the MIC.

In vivo experiments were performed on male ICR mice (5–6 weeks, weight 25–30 g) to evaluate the practical applicability of the X/N–PGQDs [20,42]. All animal experiments were approved by the Institutional Animal Care and Use Committee of the National Laboratory Animal Center, Taipei, Taiwan (IACUC Approval No. 107025) and carried out following the strict Guidelines for Care and Use of Laboratory Animals of National Taiwan Ocean University. The mice were anesthetized with isoflurane and a wound of diameter 1 cm was created on the dorsum skin, after shaving and disinfecting with 70% ethanol. 10 μL of bacterial suspension (methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA)) containing 1 × 107 CFU in sterilized saline was added to the wound to induce infection, and covered with sterilized cotton moistened with PBS to ensure bacterial growth. Samples were taken from the wound spots three days after the surgery and the infection was confirmed by cell culture on LB agar plates, incubated at 37 °C for 12 h. The antibacterial agent [50 μL of Cl/N–PGQDs-270 (50 μg mL−1)] was applied to the infected wounds without and with exposure to LED light (350 W m−2) for 1 min, covered with a gauze (1.0 cm × 1.0 cm) and fixed with an elastic adhesive bandage. Samples from the wound site were taken on the same day, to check for the antibacterial activity of the Cl/N–PGQDs-270.

See the Supporting Information for the chemicals used and experimental details on zone of inhibition, ROS, SYTO 9/PI staining, comet assay, cytotoxicity, and hemolysis assays, and TEM images for bacteria.

The X/N-PGQDs were synthesized by the direct pyrolysis of a mixture of spermidine and hydrohalic acid (HX; X = Cl, Br, I) with a HX-to-spermidine molar ratio of 3 at 210, 240, 270, and 300 °C for 3 h; denoted as X/N-PGQDs-210, X/N-PGQDs-240, X/N-PGQDs-270, X/N-PGQDs-300, respectively. The carbonized products thus obtained were sonicated for 1 h by dispersing in DI water, followed by centrifugation to remove the larger particles. The black solid pyrolytic products obtained at 300 °C were almost insoluble in water due to overcarbonization. The X/N-PGQDs suspensions were further purified through dialysis and the product yields were calculated. The yields of the X/N-PGQDs synthesized at different temperatures are shown in Table S1 (Supporting Information). Compared to the products obtained from doping with Cl or Br, the yields of I/N-PGQDs were relatively low (<0.01%) after heating above 210 °C. Due to the formation of insoluble black carbon materials and low yield (<0.01%) (Fig. S1, Supporting Information), we did not characterize the Cl/N-PGQDs and Br/N-PGQDs synthesized at 300 °C and the I/N-PGQDs synthesized at temperatures >210 °C. The TEM images show that the size of the Cl/N-PGQD, Br/N-PGQDs, and I/N-PGQDs synthesized at 210–270 °C, 210–270 °C, and 210 °C were ca. 10–125 nm, 30–125 nm, and 35 nm, respectively (Fig. 2 A). The HRTEM images (insets to Fig. 2A) show d-spacing values of 0.21 nm, 0.26 nm, and 0.34 nm for PGQDs, corresponding to the diffraction planes of (100), (020), and (002) facets [43], respectively, indicating that graphitic structures are formed during the carbonization process.

The formation of PGQDs by the heating method occurs mainly through dehydration, polymerization, crosslinking, and partial carbonization [44]. The PGQDs are comprised of abundant functional groups on their polymeric surface and graphene-like structures in the core, which are different from carbon dots that possess a spherical shape and an obvious crystal lattice [42–47]. During heating, polymers are first formed through the halide-catalyzed condensation of spermidine. The polymers are further crosslinked into gel-like nanoparticles. Subsequently, the particle size gradually reduced and a number of layered graphene dots are formed in the interior region by partial carbonization [45–50]. Thus, the PGQDs consist of a non-complete carbonized core surrounded by partly dehydrated, cross-linked, close-knit polymeric frames. The nitrogen and halogen are doped into the PGQDs during the polymerization and carbonization processes (Table S2, Supporting Information). The heteroatom doping, size, and graphitization degree of the PGQDs depend on the conditions of dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization [44,50]. Increasing the heating temperature leads to higher condensation and carbonization and, therefore, the formation of smaller particle sizes [51]. For example, the average particle size of the Cl/N-PGQDs decreases from 125 nm to 10 nm when synthesized at 210 °C versus 270 °C along with prominent graphene-like structures (Fig. 2A). This decrease in particle size of Cl/N-PGQDs with increase in temperature is supported by the DLS measurements, which show a decrease in hydrodynamic diameter from 130 nm to 22 nm (Table S3, Supporting Information). The AFM image of Cl/N-PGQDs synthesized at 270 °C also shows that the size is around 10 nm (Fig. S2, Supporting Information). Br/N-PGQDs-240 and I/N-PGQDs-210 possess an average size of 55 nm and 75 nm, respectively, as evident from AFM images.

An absorption band around 270 nm in the UV–vis absorption spectra for all of the X/N-PGQDs is due to the π→π∗ transitions of aromatic CC bonds, and a shoulder band at 300–450 nm corresponds to the n→π∗ transitions of CO and CN bonds or other connected groups (Fig. 2B) [52]. As the synthesis temperature increases from 210 to 270 °C the fluorescence intensity of the Cl/N−PGQDs also increase, which may be due to the formation of more crystalline structures in the core or edge defects at the graphene core interface and the polymeric shell (Fig. 2C) [53]. The formation of the condense polymeric shell may also contribute to the stronger fluorescence [46,47,51]. A slight redshift from 420 nm to 430 nm observed with increase in temperature from 210 to 270 °C also indicates the increase in the degree of carbonization [53]. The fluorescence shift and increase in its intensity may also be due to the superposition of multiple fluorophores in the PGQDs coupled with the low transition energy of the surface defects on the carbon skeleton [53,54]. However, the Br/N-PGQDs synthesized at 270 °C show a decrease in absorbance and fluorescence intensity compared to those synthesized at 240 °C, mainly due to overpyrolysis, thereby reducing the amount of fluorescent defect states on the surfaces [53,54]. The Cl/N-PGQDs exhibit excitation wavelength-dependent fluorescence emission properties (Fig. S3, Supporting Information), which may be due to the varying size distribution of the particles, surface defects, or formation of polycyclic aromatic clusters with different sizes [55]. We also noted that the fluorescence intensity of Cl/N–PGQDs synthesized at higher temperatures is stronger than that of Br/N–PGQDs and I/N–PGQDs, which is probably because of the high amount of chlorine and nitrogen doping. Other reports have shown that halogen doping could significantly change the electronic structure of graphene-like materials [56–58]. Also, nitrogen-doping can enhance the fluorescence properties of the carbon dots significantly [50,54]. It can create radiative recombination centers by eliminating the groups such as epoxy and carboxyl which act as non-radiative electron-hole recombination centers [50,54]. The lower fluorescent quantum yield (QY) of the I/N−PGQDs (3.84%) compared to Cl/N−PGQDs (3.85–6.13%) may be attributed to the heavy atom effect [59] (Table S2).

Elemental analysis revealed that the carbon content of the Cl/N-PGQDs synthesized at 210, 240, and 270 °C was 26.4, 30.2, and 37.5%, respectively (Table S2). All of the X/N-PGQDs exhibited higher carbon content and lower oxygen content with an increase in temperature, indicating a higher degree of carbonization and mild oxidation of the polyamine at higher temperatures [60]. The chlorine content in the Cl/N-PGQDs synthesized at 210, 240 and 270 °C, as determined by inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS), was approximately 32.7, 30.0 and 23.7%, respectively (Table S2). High-temperature pyrolysis may cause the decomposition of the C–Cl and N–Cl bonds and, therefore, lower chlorine preservation in the Cl/N-PGQDs. The oxygen and chlorine content are also correlated with the zeta potential values of the Cl/N-PGQDs. The positive zeta potential increases with the decrease in oxygen and chlorine content (Table S3, Supporting Information), which is attributed to the elimination of highly electronegative chlorine and oxygen atoms.

X-ray diffraction (XRD), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) were performed to further elucidate the structural properties of the X/N-PGQDs. A broad band at ca. 26° (2θ) in the XRD pattern of the X/N-PGQDs (Fig. S4, Supporting Information), corresponds to graphitic interlayer (002) spacing of 0.34 nm (Fig. 2A), that originates from the partial carbonization that resulted in the formation of several graphene layers [45–48]. By XPS, deconvolution of the C 1s spectrum (Fig. S5, Supporting Information) of the X/N-PGQDs shows a pronounced peak at 284.7 eV (CC), which supports the formation of graphitic structures in the PGQDs. A weak peak at 287.0 eV is due to the C–O groups on the PGQDs. The fitting peaks at 287.2 eV and 285.2 eV are due to C–Cl and C–N bonds [61,62]. The O 1s spectra show a dominant peak at 532.8 eV in all of the X/N-PGQDs, which corresponds to the C–O groups. The weak peak for CO appears at 533 eV. The presence of CO and C–O bonds shows that the X/N-PGQDs are functionalized with multiple oxygenated groups [63]. The N 1s spectra of X/N-PGQDs show the presence of both graphitic nitrogen (401.2 eV) and pyrrolic nitrogen (399.9 eV), with a higher percentage of nitrogen existing in the graphitic form. The binding energy observed at 197.5 eV and 199.1 eV for Cl 2p in Cl/N-PGQDs is due to Cl 2p3/2 and Cl 2p1/2, and corresponds to C–Cl bonds that support the doping of Cl into PGQDs [64]. Br doping is evident from the Br 3d spectra showing prominent peaks at 68.4 eV, 68.4 eV, and 69.4 eV, due to Br 3d5/2, Br 3d3/2, and Br 3d1/2, respectively. In the case of I/N-PGQDs, binding energy at 618.7 eV and 630.2 eV corresponds to I 3d5/2 and I 3d3/2 respectively. These results are consistent with the FT-IR spectra (Fig. S6, Supporting Information), which confirms the doping of nitrogen and halogen into the PGQDs. The Cl/N-PGQDs synthesized at 210, 240 and 270 °C show N–H stretching and NH2 stretching around 3300−3500 cm−1 and CH2 stretching at 2936 cm−1. The broad peak near 1600 cm−1 could have contributions from NH2 bending, as well as CO stretching and CC stretching [64]. Collectively, these support that the Cl/N-PGQDs retain some of the features of the polyamine as well as the formation of graphitic structures with oxygenated groups. The broad peak around 540−785 cm−1 is likely due to the C–Cl stretching vibration, which further supports the incorporation of chlorine atoms into the PGQDs.

Spermidine is one of the major polyamines found in bacteria, responsible for various internal cell mechanisms, and cellular metabolic processes [65]. Reports reveal that positively charged nanomaterials can directly interact with negatively charged bacterial membranes (membrane potential ca. −100 to −150 mV), thereby disrupting the integrity of the membrane [19,66,67]. Spermidine-based carbon dots with a very high positive charge (ζ = +45∼+61 mV) have been reported to possess antibacterial properties [19,20]. They can be further improved by tuning their core structure and surface properties [26]. Heteroatom doping of carbon dots with phosphorus, nitrogen or sulfur creates new defective sites, which enhances the photoluminescence intensity, and allows for their easy functionalization [68]. Carbon dots with quaternary ammonium groups are reported as a promising antibacterial agent for treating infections of drug-resistant bacteria [69]. Ran et al. also report that CQDs with long alkyl chain-linked quaternary ammonium groups are effective in eradicating biofilms of both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [70]. On the other hand, nitrogen-doping improves photostability and boosts the generation of ROS, thereby contributing to a higher antibacterial effect [23,25,35]. Chlorine-doped GQDs are also shown to have improved antibacterial activity when compared to GQDs alone, due to their singlet oxygen-generating ability [32].

The above results suggest that our Cl/N−PGQDs-270, which have high antibacterial activity when coupled with simple LED light irradiation, can effectively eradicate both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria (Table 1, Fig. 3 and Fig. S7). Unlike nitrogen- and halogen-doped carbon dots that have a size smaller than 10 nm, our X/N-PGQDs have larger sizes (>10 nm), flexible polymeric surfaces, and photo-induced radical generation characteristics that endow them possess superior photobactericidal activity. Additionally, the positive charge on the X/N−PGQDs (zeta potential >+23 mV; Table S3), which arises as a result of the cationic features of the polymeric spermidine-like structure on their surfaces, allows them for multivalent interaction with bacteria, thereby leading to the destabilization of the bacterial membranes. Cl/N−PGQDs-270 (MIC 0.005–0.05 μg mL−1) and Br/N−PGQDs-240 (MIC 0.008–0.05 μg mL−1) exhibit high zeta potential values of +40.9 and + 38.8 mV, respectively, further leading them to exhibit superior antibacterial activity relative to other X/N−PGQDs. Furthermore, we evaluated the stability of the Cl/N-PGQDs-270 (100 μg mL−1) in PBS, by DLS measurement. The zeta potential of the Cl/N-PGQDs-270 was ca. +41 mV in 5 mM sodium phosphate (pH 7.4), and it decreased to ca. +10 mV in PBS. However, the hydrodynamic diameter of the particles in PBS (25.1 ± 7.3 nm; n = 3) was only slightly larger than that in 5 mM sodium phosphate (pH 7.4) (22.1 ± 4.2 nm; n = 3), indicating Cl/N-PGQDs-270 are stable (no aggregation) in the physiological buffer solution. The Cl/N-PGQDs-270 solution stored at 4 °C for three months exhibited the same photodynamic antibacterial activity as that of the freshly prepared one.

We next explored the mechanism that endows our X/N−PGQDs with their superior antimicrobial activity relative to that of free spermidine. The positively charged X/N−PGQDs can directly interact with the cell membrane of bacteria, thereby disrupting the membrane’s integrity. The physical interaction of X/N−PGQDs with the bacterial cell membrane, along with ROS generation plays a major role in the antibacterial action. Bright-field and fluorescence images of E. coli and S. aureus after just 1 min incubation with X/N−PGQDs show obvious aggregation of bacteria (Fig. 4 ), revealing the strong interaction between the polymeric structures of the highly positively charged X/N−PGQDs and the bacterial membrane. Among the tested X/N−PGQDs, Cl/N−PGQDs-270 caused the largest aggregation of bacteria. The superior antibacterial ability of these Cl/N−PGQDs-270 may be due to their strong interaction with bacteria and their high charge-transfer ability to the bacterial membrane, which disturbs the respiratory chain and cellular metabolism. Zone of inhibition measurements further revealed that the cellulose thin film loaded with Cl/N−PGQDs-270 (diameter = 6 mm) exhibited a larger inhibition zone (19 mm) for S. aureus than those of Br/N-PGQDs-240 (16 mm) and I/N-PGQDs-210 (15 mm) (Fig. S8, Supporting Information).

TEM imaging of E. coli and S. aureus shows that treatment with X/N−PGQDs (50 μg mL−1) for 1 h caused significant bacterial membrane disruption (Fig. 5 ). Previous reports showed that polyamines play a crucial role in the stabilization of bacterial membrane structures [73,74]. About 5–10% of bacterial polyamines are adsorbed onto its membrane, and about 1% are covalently bound to its peptidoglycans [75]. However, X/N−PGQDs interact with the bacterial membrane surface and cause significant damage thereon. Reports reveal that ROS could be generated through the energy transfer between GQDs and oxygen molecules [76]. Also, the ROS generation efficiency of Cl-doped carbon dots is higher than that of carbon dots alone [32]. The antibacterial activity of nitrogen and chlorine co-doped PGQDs may be due to the higher number of defective sites on the PGQDs, which augment their interaction with oxygen molecules, and in turn, the generation of ROS [23,25,32,35]. In addition to the halogen atoms, the presence of amine and carbonyl functional groups on the polymeric surfaces of the X/N−PGQDs contribute to their interaction with bacterial membranes and enhance ROS generation, and consequently, the antibacterial activity [35]. In addition to the light-induced ROS generation, light-driven photobactericidal activity may also be due to the ROS-independent oxidative stress arising from increased charge-transfer [32]. However, the X/N−PGQDs possess a negligible photothermal effect on the inactivation of bacteria. Irradiation of X/N−PGQDs (50 μg mL−1) solution with white LED light (350 W m−2) for 1 min just increases the temperature from 25 to 30 °C; therefore, antibacterial activity due to photothermal effect can be ruled out.

The efficacy of X/N−PGQDs for light-assisted inactivation of bacterial cells was evaluated by the photodynamic index (PDI; MIC ratio of dark control versus light irradiation) (Table 1). The positively charged X/N−PGQDs (ζ>+40 mV) have a stronger electrostatic interaction toward Gram-negative bacteria than toward Gram-positive bacteria, contributing to higher antibacterial activity and hence the difference in PDI values. Moreover, the different cell membrane property of Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria also plays a role in interacting with the X/N−PGQDs. Gram-positive bacteria possess a cell wall containing thick peptidoglycan layers and teichoic acids, whereas Gram-negative bacteria possess an outer membrane containing lipopolysaccharides and phospholipids, in addition to the peptidoglycan layer. The teichoic acid may prevent the X/N−PGQDs to penetrate into the membrane, whereas the lipopolysaccharides present on the surface of Gram-negative bacteria interact with the X/N−PGQDs, and therefore enhance them to penetrate through the membrane [17]. The Cl/N−PGQDs-270 show the lowest MIC value as well as a superior PDI among the tested X/N−PGQDs. Therefore, we further investigated the antimicrobial mechanism of Cl/N−PGQDs-270 coupled with visible light irradiation-assisted inhibition of E. coli. The antibacterial activity of Cl/N−PGQDs-270 via the production of ROS within the E. coli cells was demonstrated by assessment with a 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) assay. The fluorescent product, 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCF; λ em max ≈ 530 nm), formed by the reaction of DCFH-DA with cellular esterases and intracellular ROS, was observed in Cl/N−PGQDs-treated E. coli and under LED irradiation (Fig. S9, Supporting Information). The ROS generation from Cl/N−PGQDs-treated E. coli and irradiation with LED light is obviously higher than the dark control, and even compared to treatment with H2O2 (1.0 mM). Thus, as a result of nitrogen/halogen co-doping, the Cl/N−PGQDs may possess enzyme-like activity for intracellular ROS generation under light irradiation [77]. Moreover, the nitrogen/halogen co-doping in the PGQDs increases the number of defective sites and elevates charge separation on the adjacent carbon atoms, which promotes charge-transfer and thereby the ROS-generating ability [78]. The photo-irradiation boosts the generation of ROS, which in turn initiates oxidative damage of the cytoplasmic membrane and DNA of the bacterial cells. The oxidative damage of the cytoplasmic membrane is evident from the SYTO 9/propidium iodide (PI) staining assay (Fig. S10, Supporting Information) [19]. The Cl/N−PGQDs-270 coupled with LED-irradiation treated group show cells with red fluorescence, indicating the compromised cell membrane in the case of both E. coli and S. aureus. However, DNA damage was not observed when E. coli interacts with Cl/N−PGQDs. The result of comet assay shows clear circles for control and the samples treated with Cl/N-PGQDs-270, revealing that photo-induced ROS did not induce significant genotoxicity to bacterial cells (Fig. S11, Supporting Information). The alkaline comet assay is a simple test to detect a broad spectrum of DNA lesions and alkali-labile sites in eukaryotic cells. DNA damage was observed in H2O2-treated cells, as evident from the migration of DNA away from the nucleus, forming a shape resembling a comet tail. In contrast, Cl/N−PGQDs did not induce DNA breakage in bacterial cells detected by the comet assay.

In order to identify the type of ROS generated by the Cl/N−PGQDs-270 upon light irradiation, we performed electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) measurements. We recorded the EPR spectrum of X/N−PGQDs (100 μg mL−1) containing 5,5-dimethyl pyridine N-oxide (DMPO) as the spin trap under xenon lamp (450 W) irradiation (Fig. S12, Supporting Information). The most intense signal, with the ratio of 1:2:2:1, is attributed to DMPO−⋅OH adduct (aN = aH β = 14.9 G), due to the reaction of dissolved O2 with X/N−PGQDs to produce ⋅OH free radicals [71]. We also noted that longer exposure to light irradiation significantly increases the ⋅OH radical production. The strongest intensity from the Cl/N−PGQDs-270 after light irradiation was observed. This indicates that the higher amount of ⋅OH radicals generated by Cl/N−PGQDs-270 is due to their larger surface area and number of defective sites, relative to Br/N−PGQDs-240 and I/N−PGQDs-210, which allow them to interact with oxygen molecules for the generation of ROS. Surface defects, such as interstitial oxygen, nitrogen, and chlorine defects, are reported to be an important factor for ROS generation [32,35]. The energy transfer between adsorbed oxygen molecules and carbon materials can also produce ROS [79]. Cl-doping increases the polarity of the PGQDs and enhances chemisorption of oxygen molecules and, therefore, ROS generation [63]. Furthermore, the generated ROS-induced membrane damage of E. coli caused by the Cl/N−PGQDs was demonstrated by using a lipid peroxidation-staining dye, C11-BODIPY581/591. The fluorescent fatty acid analogue of C11-BODIPY581/591 is an effective tracer of lipid trafficking and is used to assess the lipid oxidative potential of bacterial membranes [80]. The oxidation of the polyunsaturated butadienyl residue of C11-BODIPY581/591 results in the fluorescence emission band shifts from ∼590 nm to ∼510 nm and strong green fluorescence emission. The lipid peroxidation of E. coli was observed after treatment with Cl/N−PGQDs and with LED irradiation (Fig. S13, Supporting Information). Our results suggest that the Cl/N−PGQDs synthesized at 270 °C possess superior antibacterial activity that can not only be ascribed to their high surface charges that mediate their contact with the bacterial membrane, but also due to the increased levels of intracellular ROS and oxidative stress that result in damage of the bacterial cells. The complex antibacterial mechanisms of the X/N−PGQDs suggest that they have great potential to reduce the risk caused by emerging antibiotic-resistant bacterial strains.

To determine the concentration at which the functional X/N−PGQDs could be used without significant side effects, we investigated the cytotoxicity of Cl/N–PGQDs-270, Br/N–PGQDs-240 and I/N–PGQDs-210 toward HaCaT (immortalized human keratinocyte cell line) cells. Owing to the long-lived, spontaneously immortalized human keratinocyte line, HaCaT cells are widely used in scientific research for the characterization of human keratinocyte. Cytotoxicity was assessed using the Prestoblue cell-viability assay. We examined the cell viability of HaCaT cells treated with Cl/N–PGQDs-270, Br/N–PGQDs-240 or I/N–PGQDs-210 at different concentrations (1–50 μg mL−1) (Fig. S14, Supporting Information). The X/N−PGQDs are scarcely toxic to the cells (cell viability >90%) at concentration which is 10000-fold higher (50 μg mL−1) than the lowest MIC value for the tested bacteria. Furthermore, no significant hemolytic reaction was observed, even with up to 50 μg mL−1 (Fig. S15, Supporting Information). Our results reveal that the X/N−PGQDs exhibit significant antibacterial activity, while displaying negligible cytotoxicity toward mammalian cells, as well as no hemolytic effect toward red blood cells (RBCs), which is indispensable for a potential photobactericidal candidate. It is important to consider that bacterial and mammalian cells differ widely in lipid membrane composition and transmembrane potential. Bacterial membranes are composed of negatively charged phospholipids, whereas the mammalian cells possess fewer charges and mainly consist of cholesterol and phospholipids. The interaction of the highly positively charged X/N−PGQDs with the negatively charged bacterial membrane is faster when compared with the mammalian cells [20], and therefore, the mammalian cells have higher tolerance toward the X/N−PGQDs.

Although caused due to polymicrobial infections, S. aureus plays a major role in chronic wounds, as well as causing significant delay in wound healing [81]. S. aureus is the leading cause of postsurgical infections and causes dangerous infection of the skin and soft tissues. The presence of bacteria in the wound may cause tissue damage by further colonization, thereby resulting in severe infection and formation of biofilm [82]. Moreover, the emergence of multidrug-resistant strains has complicated the treatment of S. aureus infections. The use of GQDs band-aids in combination with a very low dose of H2O2 has been reported to enhance the wound healing in S. aureus infected wounds in mice [83]. To validate the practical application of the X/N−PGQDs, we tested the efficacy of X/N−PGQDs for healing MRSA infected skin wounds of rats. First, we contrast the pictures of postoperative wound healing in ICR mice treated with Cl/N−PGQDs-270 (50 μg mL−1) versus untreated wounds (Fig. 6 A). After a 5-day treatment, the wound of the light-irradiated group has healed almost completely (∼10% wound crust) when compared to the non-irradiated (∼39%) and the control groups (∼58%; treated with PBS only and without irradiation) (Fig. 6B). This result agrees well with the culture of tissue from the infected wound site. After 3 days of therapy, the group treated with Cl/N−PGQDs-270 exposed to light (350 W m−2) irradiation for 1 min showed superior antibacterial activity toward MRSA in infected tissues compared to the group exposed to Cl/N−PGQDs-270 without light exposure (Fig. 6C). The culture from the Cl/N−PGQDs-270-treated group with LED irradiation exhibited a significant reduction in the number of bacterial colonies (∼92%) (Fig. 6C). Cl/N−PGQDs-270 showed superior wound healing ability when compared to GQDs functionalized with chitosan oligosaccharide, which was used in treating MRSA infected wound by the daily administration of 10 μg mL−1 with 10 min light irradiation (450 nm, 14.2 mW cm−2) for 14 days for complete healing [84]. However, complete wound healing was observed for the light-irradiated Cl/N−PGQDs-270 in 7 days.

To facilitate wound healing, antibacterial nanomaterials should be capable of minimizing infections and increase the tissue rehydration at the wounded part [42]. Upon irradiation of the Cl/N−PGQDs-270 with LED light, the generation of ROS boosts their antibacterial effects, thereby promoting wound healing through rapid sterilization. On the other hand, to accelerate wound healing, promoting the skin cells to actively participate in skin regeneration is important [85]. ROS, in minimum amounts, have been reported as important mediators of cell signaling and wound repair [86]. Also, the production of ROS by inflammatory cells and other types of cells in the wound is essential for defense against invading bacteria [87]. In the presence of ⋅OH radicals generated by the Cl/N−PGQDs-270 under the LED irradiation, neutrophils and macrophages could modulate the activity of cells (a phenomenon described as ‘respiratory burst’), such as monocytes and platelets, which stimulates the immune response against pathogens [88]. Our results suggest that X/N−PGQDs can be a potential antibacterial material to prevent microbial colonization and reduce sepsis due to bacteria-infected wounds.

In this study, we have demonstrated the efficiency of halogen/nitrogen co-doped polymeric GQDs, synthesized by a simple heating method, for the effective inhibition of Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. The inhibitory efficacy of X/N−PGQDs toward the different bacterial strains tested is elevated upon irradiation with LED light for just 1 min. Relative to its precursor, free spermidine, the enhanced photobactericidal activity of the Cl/N–PGQDs-270 at a very low concentration (<0.05 μg mL−1) upon light treatment is due to the photodynamic effect of ROS generation combined with their strong interaction with the bacterial membrane. In vivo studies with MRSA-infected wound healing in mice revealed that light-irradiation with Cl/N–PGQDs-270 treatment resulted in better healing and excellent inhibition of bacteria. Therefore, we conclude that X/N-PGQDs are promising photobactericidal agents for the treatment of infected wounds and skin infections.
